title: Will Tariff Volatility Derail Paradigm C?

And the market has really gotten used to
the idea of tariffs.
>> Oh yeah.
>> Is this just, you know, posturing? What
do you think?
>> So, uh, thanks for having me.
>> I'm gonna tell you guys and the fine
viewers of Yahoo Finance, the same thing
we've been coaching our global investor
community on since we turned bullish in
April, which is to put ear muffs on
whenever you hear the word tariffs. This
is an administration that understands
that it has to outgrow the debt deficit
trajectory. And in our view, they're
pulling many levers both in front of us
and behind the scenes to create a
durable and robust recovery in the
economy starting in next year. And so
anything we see with regards to tariffs
and and obviously today's truth social
post um catalyzing the downturn in
markets today, in our view, it's a
buying opportunity.
>> Why isn't it a bigger problem if or do
you just think that the president's not
going to go through with this? Do you
think even if he does, it's still in
service of catalyzing that growth that
you're talking about?
>> Yeah, that's a great that's a great
question. And in our view, we think the
net impact of the fiscal, regulatory,
trade policy, monetary policy changes
that the administration is enacting with
the biggest being the likely structural
regime change we're going to see at the
Federal Reserve in the coming one to two
years. We think that the net the net mix
of that is likely to create a robust and
durable recovery in the economy. And so
if we focus myopically on one of those
aspects, then we're ultimately going to
miss the big picture. Tariffs are the
trees. What we've been saying is
paradigm C is the forest.
>> Something really interesting that you
just said is you were listing the
changes the the administration is
making. You put monetary policy under
there.
>> Absolutely.
>> Monetary policy usually doesn't go under
the administration bucket, Darius.
>> No, it does not.
>> Do you think it should?
>> Uh I don't that's not for me to opine
on. It's for me to make money on and
help our global investor community make
money. And so whether we like it or not,
it looks like that's what's happening.
In other words, and so what does that
imply then for that piece of the
stimulus uh that you're talking about?
>> Well, it's very likely that the next Fed
chair comes in and create and creates a
very robust discussion around what the
level of the neutral policy rate should
be. Uh in our opinion, we think it's
likely to be much lower is obviously
evidenced by the signaling that we
received from the administration. And we
would actually support that. Our
research and our models have
consistently shown that this is an
economy experiencing fiscal dominance
from a fiscal policy perspective, but
it's not necessarily getting the
monetary support that would offset the
distributional impact on the negative
distributional impacts on the economy
from the fiscal dominance. And so it's
necessary, a necessary precondition of
this sustained fiscal dominance regime
is monetary support, financial
oppression, monetary debasement. And in
our view, those things are going to come
regardless of how we get there. In our
opinion, they're accelerating the get
there part. Does that mean we don't
worry about the dual mandate anymore?
It's more about those sort of structural
supports.
>> Well, so again, one of the things we've
we've coached our investor community to
pay attention to is that the policy
dynamic and the discourse around policy
in this country is shifting from what
had been, you know, a focus on aggregate
economic statistics, GDP, corporate
profits, stock market to distributional
statistics. I mean, you go back and you
look at the hue of the electoral college
map from last fall. That should tell you
everything you need to know about the
sense of urgency on boots on the ground
with regards to consumers and
businesses, the small businesses that
have been left behind, the households
that have been left behind in this
economy. I mean, I'll throw a couple
statistics at you. One, we have negative
job growth in non-government and
government adjacent sectors in the
economy over the last three years. I
would say somewhere around 20% only 20%
of jobs have come from the private
sector over the last three years.
What has happened thus far this year
though has not helped those households
or that those small businesses.
>> Agreed.
>> So what changes in the next year?
>> Yeah. So so the on the positive side of
the ledger we have a significant fiscal
easing that's likely to come down the
pike starting in 26 and 27 right about
$400 billion of incremental deficit
expansion in 26 right around5 to $600
billion in 27. And so that's that's our
models are pointing to those numbers. So
that's one. On the on the deregulation
side, we're already starting to see a
structural a cyclical upturn in credit
growth. Um we're back to a trend level
of credit growth after being, you know,
hundreds of basis points below trend for
multiple years. And so we're starting to
see the deregulation both within and
external from the financial sector
really start to impact the economy. And
the third leg of that stool, the
positive side of the stool is the
structural regime change we're likely to
see at the Federal Reserve next year.
>> What could go wrong?
>> What could go wrong? Well, if they start
kicking the legs kicking over the legs
of the stool or if they overweight the
tariff piece, it's our view that their
goal is to outgrow the debt and deficit
trajectory. And so, if that's your goal,
work backwards from there. And so,
ultimately, the net of all this policy
change is likely to be positive. And
this is why we've been so bullish all
year.
